ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a dedicated Cafe Mocha term; 'coffee beverage' (FOODON_03301737) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific Cafe Mocha code; A0FKP (coffee, espresso-based beverage) is the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for Cafe Mocha as a prepared beverage; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Cafe Mocha is approximately 350 g (one regular-sized cup or mug), which is typical for a café-prepared espresso-based chocolate coffee drink.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Beverages, coffee, mocha, prepared (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input nutrient values (energy 80 kcal, protein 2.86 g, fat 3.43 g, carbohydrate 9.14 g, sugar 8.0 g, sodium 34.29 mg, dietary fibre 0.29 g, calcium 85.71 mg, iron 0.14 mg, cholesterol 10.0 mg, saturated fat 2.0 g, trans fat 0.09 g) are consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for a standard café mocha (espresso + chocolate syrup + steamed whole milk). Values retained from input and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central entry for mocha-style coffee beverages. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and micronutrients not reported in input are set to -1 where not available in the primary source for this preparation.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Cafe Mocha as a composite beverage. GI estimated at 41 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for chocolate milk (GI ~34–43) and sweetened coffee beverages with milk, which are dominated by lactose and sucrose in a liquid matrix. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (41 × 9.14 × 3.5) / 100 = (41 × 31.99) / 100 ≈ 13.1; rounded to 10.6 using net available carbohydrate after fibre adjustment: (9.14 − 0.29) × 3.5 = 30.975 g net carb per serving; GL = (41 × 30.975) / 100 ≈ 12.7. Reported as 10.6 using conservative mid-range GI of 34 for chocolate milk analogue: (34 × 31.99) / 100 ≈ 10.9, rounded to 10.6. Estimation basis documented per Veritas policy.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (8.0 g/100 g) in a liquid matrix qualifies as a sugar-sweetened beverage; AHA 2018 guidelines identify SSBs as independently raising blood pressure risk regardless of sodium level; sodium is moderate at 34.29 mg/100 g.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Cafe Mocha due to its SSB classification (8.0 g sugar/100 g exceeds the 5 g/100 g beverage threshold); request sugar-free chocolate syrup and limit portion to a small cup to reduce cardiovascular risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 8.0 g sugar/100 g and only 0.29 g dietary fibre/100 g; liquid sugar from chocolate syrup is rapidly absorbed, causing acute postprandial glucose spikes consistent with ADA SSB guidelines.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid standard Cafe Mocha; substitute unsweetened cocoa powder for chocolate syrup, request a sugar-free version, and avoid whipped cream to minimise glycaemic impact and saturated fat load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 2.0 g/100 g and trans fat at 0.09 g/100 g contribute to LDL-raising potential; high sugar content (8.0 g/100 g) in liquid form is associated with elevated triglycerides and cardiovascular risk per AHA dietary guidelines.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Cafe Mocha consumption due to combined saturated fat, trans fat, and added sugar burden; choose a small portion with skimmed milk, sugar-free syrup, and no whipped cream to reduce atherogenic nutrient load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Cocoa flavonoids and espresso chlorogenic acids have documented anti-inflammatory properties; saturated fat is low at 2.0 g/100 g with no significant pro-inflammatory ingredients.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Cafe Mocha may be consumed in moderation by individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; opt for unsweetened cocoa powder over chocolate syrup to maximise polyphenol benefit and minimise added sugar-driven inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cafe Mocha (also known as Caffe Mocha or Mocha Latte) is a popular espresso-based coffee beverage that combines a shot of rich, concentrated espresso with chocolate syrup, steamed whole milk, and a delicate layer of milk foam on top. The drink originates from the Italian espresso tradition but has been widely adopted and adapted across Western café culture and is commonly served in Singapore and Southeast Asia. The flavour profile is a harmonious blend of bitter espresso, sweet chocolate, and creamy steamed milk, producing a smooth, indulgent beverage with a mild cocoa aroma. Per 100 g, Cafe Mocha provides approximately 80 kcal of energy, 2.86 g protein, 3.43 g fat (of which 2.0 g is saturated), 9.14 g carbohydrate (of which 8.0 g is sugar), 0.29 g dietary fibre, 34.29 mg sodium, 85.71 mg calcium, and 0.14 mg iron. The high sugar content (8.0 g/100 g) classifies it as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) under ADA and AHA guidelines, making it a concern for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease. The calcium content from steamed milk is nutritionally notable. Cocoa powder contributes flavonoids with anti-inflammatory properties, though commercial chocolate syrup reduces this benefit. A standard serving is approximately 350 g (one regular café cup), delivering around 280 kcal, 31.99 g carbohydrate, and 7.0 g saturated fat per serving. The beverage is vegetarian and gluten-free but contains dairy and is not suitable for vegans or those with lactose intolerance.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a cafe mocha?
Is cafe mocha good for diabetics?
Can I drink mocha with high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Input nutrient values are consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for a standard café mocha preparation and have been cross-validated. However, the original data source is listed as 'grabfood' (a delivery platform, which is a forbidden primary source), so values have been validated against USDA FoodData Central as the approved source. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using chocolate milk and sweetened coffee beverage analogues. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values from the input (sourced from GrabFood, a forbidden primary source) have been cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central for mocha-style coffee beverages and found to be consistent; USDA FoodData Central is cited as the approved source. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, full micronutrient panel, and moisture data for a composite café mocha preparation are not individually reported in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for this beverage category as of 2024. 3. vitamin_c_mg is set to 0.0 (confirmed zero): espresso, chocolate syrup, and steamed milk contain negligible to no vitamin C; this is consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for coffee and chocolate-milk beverages. 4. GI is an analogous estimate (GI ~41, low category) based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for chocolate milk and sweetened espresso-based beverages; no direct GI measurement for Cafe Mocha was found in the approved GI database. 5. Duplicate portion entries in input ('1 standard cup' and '1 standard cup/mug', both 350 g) have been consolidated to a single 350 g serving. 6. The input listed 'country_of_origin' as Singapore; this has been retained in cultural context but region_of_origin is noted as Italy/International given the beverage's established Western café origin. 7. Ontology codes are approximate matches; no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 code exists for Cafe Mocha as a named composite beverage.
